findings.suggestion: Before extracting, iterate `zf.infolist()` and reject the archive if cumulative `file_size` exceeds a total cap, if any single entry exceeds a per-file cap, or if `file_size/compress_size` exceeds a max ratio for any entry. Alternatively extract members one at a time via a length-limited streamed copy (`zf.open(...)` + bounded `read`), aborting once a byte budget is hit. Also bound the entry count. (Note: path traversal is already mitigated by CPython's extractall name sanitization, so no extra guard is needed there.)
findings.summary: source.py:9 `zf.extractall(dest)` extracts every archive member unconditionally. `zipfile` never enforces a size cap, so a small malicious archive (high-redundancy or overlapping/nested entries) decompresses to arbitrarily large output, exhausting disk/inodes (and memory for downstream readers) — a resource-exhaustion DoS. The per-entry `ZipInfo.file_size`/`compress_size` available on line 8's `zf` are never inspected before extraction.
findings.title: Zip bomb: extractall() with no uncompressed-size or compression-ratio limit

findings.suggestion: Before extracting, iterate zf.infolist(): reject if any member's file_size exceeds a per-file cap, if the sum of file_size exceeds a total cap, or if file_size/compress_size exceeds a ratio threshold. Prefer streaming each member through a bounded read (zf.open(member) with a byte counter) and abort on overrun, rather than extractall(). Also bound member count to guard against many-tiny-files bombs.
findings.summary: extract_archive calls zf.extractall(dest) at source.py:9 with no inspection of zf.infolist() member sizes, no cumulative uncompressed-byte cap, and no compression-ratio check. The BUG comment at lines 6-7 accurately describes present behavior: a small malicious archive can decompress to arbitrary size, exhausting disk and memory (CWE-409). The implicit caller assumption 'this safely handles an untrusted archive' is contradicted by the code.
findings.title: Unbounded zip extraction — decompression-bomb DoS (no size/ratio guard)

summary:
Risk-tiered review (trivial) completed.

The single 9-line change adds extract_archive, which is itself the documented vulnerability: zf.extractall (line 9) runs with no size, count, or compression-ratio limit, enabling a zip-bomb resource-exhaustion DoS. The BUG comment (lines 6-7) is confirmed accurate, not stale. Path-traversal (Zip Slip) and symlink-escape were considered and rejected — CPython's extractall sanitizes member paths and does not recreate symlinks. Function has 0 callers, so exposure is currently dormant; severity is high (not critical) on that basis. Finding is proven statically; a runtime PoC is unnecessary to confirm the missing guard.

Single newly-added function `extract_archive`. The self-documented zip-bomb vulnerability is real and confirmed against zipfile's actual behavior: extractall() applies no size or compression-ratio limit, allowing a small crafted archive to exhaust disk/memory (DoS). No path-traversal finding — CPython's extractall sanitizes member names, so that would be a false positive. 0 callers currently, but this is a reusable utility intended for untrusted input, so severity is high on the code's nature rather than current blast radius.
